examples.input: Initialize with capacity=3. Operations: insertLast(1), insertFront(2), insertLast(3), deleteFront(), insertFront(4), getRear()
examples.explanation: 1. Start: arr=[None,None,None], front=0, rear=0, size=0, cap=3. 2. insertLast(1): arr[0]=1, rear=(0+1)%3=1, size=1. 3. insertFront(2): front=(0-1)%3=2, arr[2]=2, size=2. 4. insertLast(3): arr[1]=3, rear=(1+1)%3=2, size=3. 5. deleteFront(): front=(2+1)%3=0, size=2. 6. insertFront(4): Need resize? size=2, cap=3, no. front=(0-1)%3=2, arr[2]=4, size=3. 7. getRear(): rear=2, arr[(2-1)%3]=arr[1]=3. Output: 3.

examples.input: Initialize with capacity=2. Operations: insertLast(5), insertFront(6), insertLast(7), deleteLast(), getFront()
examples.explanation: 1. Start: arr=[None,None], front=0, rear=0, size=0, cap=2. 2. insertLast(5): arr[0]=5, rear=1, size=1. 3. insertFront(6): front=(0-1)%2=1, arr[1]=6, size=2. 4. insertLast(7): size==cap, trigger resize. New cap=4, new_arr=[None]*4. Copy: old front=1, copy arr[1]=6 to new_arr[0], old rear=1, copy arr[0]=5 to new_arr[1]. Reset front=0, rear=2, size=2. Now insert 7: arr[2]=7, rear=3, size=3. 5. deleteLast(): rear=(3-1)%4=2, size=2. 6. getFront(): front=0, arr[0]=6. Output: 6.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Dynamic Array with Resizing
approaches.summary: Use a dynamic array (like Python list or Java ArrayList) to store elements, resizing when capacity is exceeded. This allows O(1) amortized time for insertions and deletions at both ends, but resizing operations are costly.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for operations, O(n) worst-case during resize

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Circular Deque with Dynamic Array and Resizing
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a circular deque using dynamic array with automatic resizing when capacity is exceeded.
ladders.explanation: A circular deque (double-ended queue) allows insertion and deletion at both ends with O(1) amortized time complexity. We implement this using a dynamic array (list) that grows when full. The key insight is maintaining two pointers (front and rear) that wrap around using modulo arithmetic, creating a circular buffer effect. When the array becomes full, we resize it to double capacity and reorder elements to maintain the circular structure. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) random access, and by making them circular, we avoid expensive shifting operations. The resizing ensures we don't run out of space, though it costs O(n) when triggered. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize array with initial capacity, set front=0, rear=0, size=0. 2) For insertFront: decrement front modulo capacity, place element, increment size. 3) For insertLast: place element at rear, increment rear modulo capacity, increment size. 4) For deleteFront: increment front modulo capacity, decrement size. 5) For deleteLast: decrement rear modulo capacity, decrement size. 6) Check resize when size==capacity: create new array with double capacity, copy elements from front to end then beginning to rear-1, reset front=0, rear=size. Time: O(1) amortized (resizing occurs rarely, spreading cost). Space: O(n). Connection: This combines all lower-level concepts into a complete data structure. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty/full conditions, incorrect modulo arithmetic, not resetting pointers after resize. Real code pattern: class CircularDeque with methods __init__, insertFront, insertLast, deleteFront, deleteLast, getFront, getRear, isEmpty, isFull, _resize.

examples.input: Initialize with capacity=3. Operations: insertLast(1), insertFront(2), insertLast(3), deleteFront(), insertFront(4), getRear()
examples.trace: 1. Start: arr=[None,None,None], front=0, rear=0, size=0, cap=3. 2. insertLast(1): arr[0]=1, rear=(0+1)%3=1, size=1. 3. insertFront(2): front=(0-1)%3=2, arr[2]=2, size=2. 4. insertLast(3): arr[1]=3, rear=(1+1)%3=2, size=3. 5. deleteFront(): front=(2+1)%3=0, size=2. 6. insertFront(4): Need resize? size=2, cap=3, no. front=(0-1)%3=2, arr[2]=4, size=3. 7. getRear(): rear=2, arr[(2-1)%3]=arr[1]=3. Output: 3.

examples.input: Initialize with capacity=2. Operations: insertLast(5), insertFront(6), insertLast(7), deleteLast(), getFront()
examples.trace: 1. Start: arr=[None,None], front=0, rear=0, size=0, cap=2. 2. insertLast(5): arr[0]=5, rear=1, size=1. 3. insertFront(6): front=(0-1)%2=1, arr[1]=6, size=2. 4. insertLast(7): size==cap, trigger resize. New cap=4, new_arr=[None]*4. Copy: old front=1, copy arr[1]=6 to new_arr[0], old rear=1, copy arr[0]=5 to new_arr[1]. Reset front=0, rear=2, size=2. Now insert 7: arr[2]=7, rear=3, size=3. 5. deleteLast(): rear=(3-1)%4=2, size=2. 6. getFront(): front=0, arr[0]=6. Output: 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Resize and Reorder Array for Circular Buffer
ladders.desc: Implement the resize function that doubles capacity and maintains circular order when array is full.
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function that enables the amortized O(1) time complexity. When the dynamic array becomes full (size == capacity), we need to expand it while preserving the circular order of elements. The intuition: simply creating a larger array and copying elements in their logical order (front to rear) simplifies subsequent operations. Without proper reordering, the circular pointers would become invalid. Step-by-step: 1) Create new array with double the current capacity. 2) Copy elements from the old array starting at front index, wrapping around to the end, then from beginning to rear-1. This ensures elements are placed contiguously from index 0 in the new array. 3) Reset front pointer to 0 and rear pointer to size (since all elements are now at start). 4) Update capacity to new size. Time: O(n) for copying n elements. Space: O(n) for the new array. Connection: This function is called within insertFront and insertLast when the array is full, enabling the full deque implementation. Common mistakes: not handling wrap-around correctly during copy, forgetting to update pointers, or not doubling capacity (which would cause frequent resizes). Real code pattern: def _resize(self): new_cap = self.cap * 2; new_arr = [None]*new_cap; for i in range(self.size): new_arr[i] = self.arr[(self.front + i) % self.cap]; self.arr = new_arr; self.front = 0; self.rear = self.size; self.cap = new_cap.

examples.input: Array: [3,1,2] with front=2, rear=1, size=3, cap=3. Resize to cap=6.
examples.trace: 1. Current: arr indices 0:3, 1:1, 2:2. Logical order starting at front=2: arr[2]=2, then wrap: arr[0]=3, arr[1]=1. 2. Create new_arr size 6: [None]*6. 3. Copy: i=0: new_arr[0]=arr[(2+0)%3]=arr[2]=2. i=1: new_arr[1]=arr[(2+1)%3]=arr[0]=3. i=2: new_arr[2]=arr[(2+2)%3]=arr[1]=1. 4. Set front=0, rear=size=3. Result: new_arr=[2,3,1,None,None,None].

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30,40] with front=3, rear=2, size=4, cap=4. Resize to cap=8.
examples.trace: 1. Current: arr indices 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40. Logical order starting at front=3: arr[3]=40, then wrap: arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30. 2. Create new_arr size 8. 3. Copy: i=0: new_arr[0]=arr[(3+0)%4]=arr[3]=40. i=1: new_arr[1]=arr[(3+1)%4]=arr[0]=10. i=2: new_arr[2]=arr[(3+2)%4]=arr[1]=20. i=3: new_arr[3]=arr[(3+3)%4]=arr[2]=30. 4. Set front=0, rear=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circular Indexing with Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Implement circular buffer behavior using modulo operations to wrap indices around array bounds.
ladders.explanation: The central technique enabling efficient deque operations is circular indexing. Instead of physically shifting elements when adding/removing from ends, we use two pointers (front and rear) that move circularly within a fixed-size array. Modulo arithmetic ensures these pointers wrap around to the beginning when they reach the end, creating a virtual circle. Intuition: Think of the array as a circle where indices 0 and n-1 are adjacent. When front moves left past 0, it wraps to n-1; when rear moves right past n-1, it wraps to 0. This allows O(1) operations without data movement. Step-by-step: For insertFront: front = (front - 1) % capacity. For insertLast: rear = (rear + 1) % capacity. For deleteFront: front = (front + 1) % capacity. For deleteLast: rear = (rear - 1) % capacity. Always check if deque is full (size == capacity) or empty (size == 0) before operations. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This logic is used in all deque operations and feeds into the resize subroutine when array is full. Common mistakes: using incorrect modulo for negative numbers (in Python, -1 % n = n-1, which works), forgetting to update size, or confusing front/rear directions. Real code pattern: front = (front - 1) % self.cap; self.arr[front] = value; size += 1.

examples.input: Array size 4, front=0, rear=0. Perform: insertLast(A), insertLast(B), insertFront(C), deleteFront(), insertLast(D). Track indices.
examples.trace: 1. Start: cap=4, front=0, rear=0, size=0. 2. insertLast(A): arr[0]=A, rear=(0+1)%4=1, size=1. 3. insertLast(B): arr[1]=B, rear=(1+1)%4=2, size=2. 4. insertFront(C): front=(0-1)%4=3, arr[3]=C, size=3. 5. deleteFront(): front=(3+1)%4=0, size=2. 6. insertLast(D): arr[2]=D, rear=(2+1)%4=3, size=3. Final: front=0, rear=3.

examples.input: Array size 3, front=2, rear=2, size=3 (full). Perform deleteLast(), insertFront(X). Track indices.
examples.trace: 1. Start: cap=3, front=2, rear=2, size=3. 2. deleteLast(): rear=(2-1)%3=1, size=2. 3. insertFront(X): front=(2-1)%3=1, arr[1]=X, size=3. Final: front=1, rear=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Array Expansion Strategy
ladders.desc: Implement a dynamic array that doubles in size when capacity is exceeded.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating a resizable array, which is fundamental to achieving amortized O(1) time for insertions. A dynamic array starts with an initial capacity and grows geometrically (usually doubling) when full. The intuition: doubling ensures that resizes happen infrequently, so the cost of copying elements is spread over many insertions, leading to O(1) amortized time. If we increased by a fixed amount (e.g., +10), resizes would occur more often, degrading performance. Step-by-step: 1) Maintain an internal array and current size. 2) When adding an element, check if size == capacity. 3) If full, create a new array with capacity = old capacity * 2. 4) Copy all elements from old to new array. 5) Add the new element. Time: O(1) amortized (each element copied at most twice during resizes). Space: O(n). Connection: This building block is used in the resize subroutine for the circular deque. Common mistakes: not doubling (causing frequent resizes), forgetting to copy elements, or not updating capacity after resize. Real code pattern: def append(self, value): if self.size == self.cap: new_arr = [None] * (self.cap * 2); for i in range(self.size): new_arr[i] = self.arr[i]; self.arr = new_arr; self.cap *= 2; self.arr[self.size] = value; self.size += 1.

examples.input: Start with capacity=2. Append: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Track capacity changes.
examples.trace: 1. Initial: arr=[None,None], size=0, cap=2. 2. Append 1: arr[0]=1, size=1. 3. Append 2: arr[1]=2, size=2. 4. Append 3: size==cap, resize to cap=4, new_arr=[None]*4, copy 1,2 to new_arr, arr=new_arr, arr[2]=3, size=3. 5. Append 4: arr[3]=4, size=4. 6. Append 5: size==cap, resize to cap=8, new_arr=[None]*8, copy 1,2,3,4, arr=new_arr, arr[4]=5, size=5. Final: cap=8.

examples.input: Start with capacity=1. Append: A, B, C. Track capacity changes.
examples.trace: 1. Initial: arr=[None], size=0, cap=1. 2. Append A: arr[0]=A, size=1. 3. Append B: size==cap, resize to cap=2, new_arr=[None]*2, copy A, arr=new_arr, arr[1]=B, size=2. 4. Append C: size==cap, resize to cap=4, new_arr=[None]*4, copy A,B, arr=new_arr, arr[2]=C, size=3. Final: cap=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert at Both Ends of a Fixed-Size Array
ladders.desc: Implement insertion at front and rear of an array without resizing, using shifting or circular logic.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves adding elements to either end of an array. In a simple array (not circular), inserting at front requires shifting all elements right, costing O(n). Inserting at rear is O(1) if space exists. The intuition: arrays have contiguous memory, so front insertion is expensive due to shifting. For circular buffers, we avoid shifting by using pointers, but here we practice the basic concept. Step-by-step for non-circular: 1) For insertFront: Check if array has space. Shift all elements one position to the right. Place new element at index 0. 2) For insertLast: Check if array has space. Place new element at the next available index (size). Time: O(n) for front, O(1) for rear. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This operation is foundational for understanding why circular buffers are more efficient. Common mistakes: not checking bounds, incorrect shifting logic, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def insertFront(arr, size, cap, value): if size == cap: error; for i in range(size-1, -1, -1): arr[i+1] = arr[i]; arr[0] = value; size += 1.

examples.input: Array: [5,6,None,None], size=2, cap=4. insertFront(4). Result?
examples.trace: 1. Start: arr=[5,6,None,None], size=2. 2. Check: size<cap, proceed. 3. Shift elements right: i=1: arr[2]=arr[1]=6. i=0: arr[1]=arr[0]=5. 4. Insert at index 0: arr[0]=4. 5. size=3. Result: [4,5,6,None].

examples.input: Array: [1,2,3], size=3, cap=3. insertLast(4). Result?
examples.trace: 1. Start: arr=[1,2,3], size=3, cap=3. 2. Check: size==cap, array is full. Cannot insert. Return error or resize.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Understand how to access array elements by index and prevent out-of-bounds errors.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where elements are accessed via indices starting at 0. Bounds checking ensures we don't access indices outside the valid range (0 to length-1), which would cause errors. Intuition: Think of an array as a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; you can only put items in existing boxes. Step-by-step: 1) Declare an array with a fixed size. 2) To read/write an element, use array[index]. 3) Always verify that index is between 0 and array length minus 1 before access. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This foundation is used in all array operations, including the circular deque. Common mistakes: using index equal to length (off-by-one), negative indices without proper handling, or not initializing arrays. Real code pattern: if index < 0 or index >= len(arr): raise IndexError; else: return arr[index].

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30,40]. Access index 2.
examples.trace: 1. Array length = 4, valid indices: 0,1,2,3. 2. Index 2 is within bounds. 3. arr[2] = 30. Output: 30.

examples.input: Array: [5,6,7]. Access index 3.
examples.trace: 1. Array length = 3, valid indices: 0,1,2. 2. Index 3 is >= length. 3. Cannot access; raise IndexError.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Fixed-Size Circular Array
approaches.summary: Implement the deque using a fixed-size array with two pointers (front and rear) that wrap around modulo capacity. This ensures O(1) time for all operations without resizing, but requires predefined capacity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Circular Deque Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement all circular deque operations using fixed-size array with front/rear pointers
ladders.explanation: The circular deque is a double-ended queue implemented with a fixed-size array where front and rear pointers wrap around using modulo arithmetic. This design ensures O(1) time complexity for all operations while maintaining a predefined capacity. The intuition is that by treating the array as circular, we can reuse empty spaces created by deletions without shifting elements. The front pointer tracks the first element, while rear tracks the position after the last element. Empty state is when front == rear, full state is when (rear + 1) % capacity == front (leaving one empty slot to distinguish from empty). Operations: insertFront: decrement front modulo capacity, insert at new front; insertLast: insert at rear, increment rear modulo capacity; deleteFront: increment front modulo capacity; deleteLast: decrement rear modulo capacity; getFront/getRear: return elements at respective positions; isEmpty/isFull: check pointer relationships. Time: O(1) for all operations. Space: O(n) for array. Connection to L1: This combines all sub-routines into complete implementation. Common mistakes: forgetting modulo wrap-around, incorrect full/empty detection, off-by-one errors in pointer updates. Real code pattern: class CircularDeque with array, front, rear, capacity attributes and methods implementing above logic.

examples.input: Initialize capacity=3, operations: insertLast(1), insertFront(2), insertLast(3), getFront(), getRear(), deleteFront(), insertFront(4), getFront(), getRear()
examples.trace:
1. init: arr=[_,_,_], front=0, rear=0, size=0
2. insertLast(1): arr[0]=1, rear=(0+1)%3=1, arr=[1,_,_], front=0, rear=1
3. insertFront(2): front=(0-1+3)%3=2, arr[2]=2, arr=[1,_,2], front=2, rear=1
4. insertLast(3): check full: (rear+1)%3=(1+1)%3=2 == front=2 → return false
5. getFront(): arr[front]=arr[2]=2
6. getRear(): arr[(rear-1+3)%3]=arr[(1-1+3)%3=0]=1
7. deleteFront(): front=(2+1)%3=0, arr=[1,_,_], front=0, rear=1
8. insertFront(4): front=(0-1+3)%3=2, arr[2]=4, arr=[1,_,4], front=2, rear=1
9. getFront(): arr[2]=4
10. getRear(): arr[(1-1+3)%3=0]=1

examples.input: Initialize capacity=4, operations: insertFront(1), insertLast(2), insertFront(3), insertLast(4), deleteLast(), deleteFront(), insertLast(5), getFront(), getRear(), isFull()
examples.output: [true, true, true, true, true, true, true, 1, 5, true]
examples.trace:
1. init: arr=[_,_,_,_], front=0, rear=0
2. insertFront(1): front=(0-1+4)%4=3, arr[3]=1, arr=[_,_,_,1], front=3, rear=0
3. insertLast(2): arr[0]=2, rear=(0+1)%4=1, arr=[2,_,_,1], front=3, rear=1
4. insertFront(3): front=(3-1+4)%4=2, arr[2]=3, arr=[2,_,3,1], front=2, rear=1
5. insertLast(4): arr[1]=4, rear=(1+1)%4=2, arr=[2,4,3,1], front=2, rear=2 → full check: (rear+1)%4=(2+1)%4=3 != front=2, so insert
6. deleteLast(): rear=(2-1+4)%4=1, arr=[2,_,3,1], front=2, rear=1
7. deleteFront(): front=(2+1)%4=3, arr=[2,_,_,1], front=3, rear=1
8. insertLast(5): arr[1]=5, rear=(1+1)%4=2, arr=[2,5,_,1], front=3, rear=2
9. getFront(): arr[3]=1
10. getRear(): arr[(2-1+4)%4=1]=5
11. isFull(): (rear+1)%4=(2+1)%4=3 == front=3 → true

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Circular Array Insertion/Deletion with Pointer Management
ladders.desc: Implement insertFront, insertLast, deleteFront, deleteLast with circular wrap-around
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core operations of adding/removing elements from both ends while maintaining circular pointer logic. The intuition is that by using modulo arithmetic, pointers wrap around to the beginning when they reach the end, creating a circular buffer. For insertFront: first check if deque is full, if not, move front backward (front = (front - 1 + capacity) % capacity), insert at new front. For insertLast: check full, insert at rear, move rear forward (rear = (rear + 1) % capacity). For deleteFront: check empty, move front forward (front = (front + 1) % capacity). For deleteLast: check empty, move rear backward (rear = (rear - 1 + capacity) % capacity). Time: O(1) each. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: These are the main operations called by the full implementation. Connection to L2: This implements the circular technique. Common mistakes: forgetting to check full/empty, incorrect modulo with negative numbers, updating pointer before/after insertion. Real code pattern: def insertFront(value): if isFull(): return False; front = (front - 1) % capacity; arr[front] = value; return True. Similar for others with appropriate pointer updates.

examples.input: capacity=4, initial front=0, rear=0, arr=[_,_,_,_], operations: insertLast(5), insertFront(3), deleteFront(), insertLast(7)
examples.trace:
1. insertLast(5): arr[0]=5, rear=(0+1)%4=1 → arr=[5,_,_,_], front=0, rear=1
2. insertFront(3): front=(0-1+4)%4=3, arr[3]=3 → arr=[5,_,_,3], front=3, rear=1
3. deleteFront(): front=(3+1)%4=0 → arr=[5,_,_,3], front=0, rear=1
4. insertLast(7): arr[1]=7, rear=(1+1)%4=2 → arr=[5,7,_,3], front=0, rear=2

examples.input: capacity=3, initial front=2, rear=1, arr=[4,_,1], operations: deleteLast(), insertFront(9), insertLast(2), deleteFront()
examples.trace:
1. deleteLast(): rear=(1-1+3)%3=0 → arr=[4,_,1], front=2, rear=0
2. insertFront(9): front=(2-1+3)%3=1, arr[1]=9 → arr=[4,9,1], front=1, rear=0
3. insertLast(2): arr[0]=2, rear=(0+1)%3=1 → arr=[2,9,1], front=1, rear=1 (full now)
4. deleteFront(): front=(1+1)%3=2 → arr=[2,9,1], front=2, rear=1

testCases.input: capacity=3, front=0, rear=0, arr=[_,_,_], ops: insertLast(1), insertFront(2), insertLast(3)
testCases.expected: arr=[1,_,2], front=2, rear=1 (insertLast(3) fails - full detected)

testCases.input: capacity=4, front=1, rear=3, arr=[_,5,6,_], ops: deleteFront(), deleteLast(), insertFront(7)
capacity=2, front=0, rear=0, arr=[_,_], ops: insertLast(1), insertFront(2), deleteLast()
testCases.expected: arr=[2,1], front=1, rear=0 (after deleteLast, rear wraps)

testCases.input: capacity=5, front=3, rear=1, arr=[_,_,_,4,5], ops: insertLast(6), deleteFront(), insertFront(7)
capacity=3, front=2, rear=2, arr=[1,2,3], ops: deleteFront(), deleteLast(), insertLast(4)
testCases.expected: arr=[1,4,3], front=0, rear=2 (deleteFront: front→0, deleteLast: rear→1, insertLast: rear→2)

testCases.input: capacity=4, front=0, rear=0, arr=[_,_,_,_], ops: insertFront(1), insertFront(2), insertFront(3), deleteLast()
capacity=3, front=1, rear=0, arr=[5,_,4], ops: insertLast(6), deleteFront(), insertFront(7)
testCases.expected: arr=[6,7,4], front=1, rear=1 (insertLast: arr[0]=6 rear→1; deleteFront: front→2; insertFront: front→1)

testCases.input: capacity=2, front=1, rear=0, arr=[3,_], ops: deleteLast(), insertFront(8), insertLast(9)
capacity=5, front=4, rear=2, arr=[_,_,1,2,3], ops: deleteFront(), insertLast(4), deleteLast()
capacity=3, front=0, rear=2, arr=[1,2,_], ops: insertFront(3), deleteLast(), insertLast(4)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circular Buffer Technique with Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Implement circular wrap-around of indices using modulo operation
ladders.explanation: The core insight is using modulo arithmetic to create a circular array where indices wrap around to 0 when reaching capacity. This allows efficient reuse of space without shifting elements. The intuition: when an index i reaches capacity-1, instead of stopping, (i+1) % capacity gives 0, wrapping to start. Similarly, (i-1+capacity) % capacity wraps from 0 to capacity-1. This creates a virtual circle. Key applications: increment rear: rear = (rear + 1) % capacity; decrement front: front = (front - 1 + capacity) % capacity; check if next position is available. Time: O(1) for each modulo operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This technique enables the insertion/deletion operations. Connection to L3: Builds on array indexing. Common mistakes: forgetting to add capacity before modulo for negative numbers, using modulo incorrectly for boundary checks. Real code pattern: For forward wrap: next_idx = (current + 1) % n. For backward wrap: prev_idx = (current - 1 + n) % n. Example: capacity=4, current=3, next=(3+1)%4=0, prev=(3-1+4)%4=2.

examples.input: capacity=5, current index=3, operations: increment 2 times, decrement 1 time
examples.trace:
1. Start: idx=3
2. Increment 1: idx=(3+1)%5=4
3. Increment 2: idx=(4+1)%5=0
4. Decrement 1: idx=(0-1+5)%5=4
Final: idx=4

examples.input: capacity=3, current index=0, operations: decrement 2 times, increment 1 time
examples.trace:
1. Start: idx=0
2. Decrement 1: idx=(0-1+3)%3=2
3. Decrement 2: idx=(2-1+3)%3=1
4. Increment 1: idx=(1+1)%3=2
Final: idx=2

testCases.input: capacity=4, start=0, ops: inc, inc, dec
capacity=3, start=2, ops: inc, dec, inc
capacity=5, start=4, ops: inc, inc, inc
capacity=2, start=1, ops: dec, dec, inc
capacity=6, start=0, ops: dec, dec, inc, inc
capacity=4, start=3, ops: inc, dec, dec
capacity=3, start=1, ops: inc, inc, dec, dec
capacity=5, start=2, ops: dec, inc, dec, inc
capacity=2, start=0, ops: inc, dec, inc, dec
capacity=4, start=2, ops: inc, inc, dec, inc, dec

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing with Boundary Conditions
ladders.desc: Access and modify array elements with index bounds checking
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves working with fixed-size arrays, ensuring indices stay within [0, capacity-1]. The intuition: arrays have contiguous memory locations indexed from 0 to n-1; accessing outside causes errors. For circular deque, we need to: 1) Check if index is valid before access, 2) Handle wrap-around mentally. Key operations: get element at i: arr[i] if 0 ≤ i < capacity; set element at i: arr[i] = value; check if position available. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This provides the array structure that circular technique operates on. Connection to L4: Builds on basic array access. Common mistakes: accessing index = capacity (off-by-one), not initializing array properly. Real code pattern: arr = [None] * capacity; def get(index): return arr[index] if 0 <= index < capacity else error; def set(index, value): arr[index] = value.

examples.input: capacity=4, arr=[10,20,30,40], operations: get index 2, set index 1 to 25, get index 4
examples.trace:
1. get index 2: arr[2]=30
2. set index 1 to 25: arr[1]=25 → arr=[10,25,30,40]
3. get index 4: index 4 >= capacity 4 → error

examples.input: capacity=3, arr=[5,_,8], operations: set index 0 to 1, get index 1, set index 3 to 9
examples.output: arr becomes [1,_,8], get returns None (or default), error for index 3
examples.trace:
1. set index 0 to 1: arr[0]=1 → arr=[1,_,8]
2. get index 1: arr[1] is None (or default value)
3. set index 3 to 9: index 3 >= capacity 3 → error

testCases.input: capacity=2, arr=[7,3], ops: get 0, get 1, set 0 to 5
capacity=3, arr=[1,2,3], ops: get 2, set 1 to 9, get 1
capacity=4, arr=[_,_,_,_], ops: set 0 to 4, set 3 to 1, get 2
capacity=1, arr=[5], ops: get 0, set 0 to 6, get 0
capacity=5, arr=[10,20,30,40,50], ops: get 4, set 2 to 35, get 2
capacity=3, arr=[a,b,c], ops: get 0, set 1 to d, get 1
capacity=2, arr=[x,y], ops: set 1 to z, get 1, get 0
capacity=4, arr=[1,_,3,_], ops: get 2, set 3 to 4, get 3
capacity=3, arr=[p,q,r], ops: set 0 to s, set 2 to t, get 1
capacity=5, arr=[0,0,0,0,0], ops: set 0 to 1, set 4 to 5, get 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Creation and Element Assignment
ladders.desc: Create fixed-size array and assign values to specific positions
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves initializing an array of predetermined size and storing/retrieving values at indices. The intuition: an array is a contiguous block of memory that can hold multiple items; we allocate space once and then fill positions. Steps: 1) Create array: arr = [default_value] * capacity, where default_value might be None or 0. 2) Assign value: arr[index] = value. 3) Retrieve value: x = arr[index]. Time: O(1) for each access. Space: O(n) for array. Connection to L3: This is the foundation for array indexing. Connection to L5: Builds on basic data structure concept. Common mistakes: using variable size, not initializing all positions, confusing index numbering (0-based). Real code pattern: capacity = 5; arr = [None] * capacity; arr[2] = 10; value = arr[2].

examples.input: Create array size 3, assign index 0=7, index 2=9, retrieve index 1
examples.trace:
1. Create: arr = [None] * 3 → [None, None, None]
2. Assign index 0=7: arr[0]=7 → [7, None, None]
3. Assign index 2=9: arr[2]=9 → [7, None, 9]
4. Retrieve index 1: arr[1] = None

examples.input: Create array size 4 with default 0, assign index 1=5, index 3=2, retrieve index 0 and 3
examples.trace:
1. Create: arr = [0] * 4 → [0,0,0,0]
2. Assign index 1=5: arr[1]=5 → [0,5,0,0]
3. Assign index 3=2: arr[3]=2 → [0,5,0,2]
4. Retrieve index 0: arr[0]=0
5. Retrieve index 3: arr[3]=2

testCases.input: size=2, assign: 0=1, 1=2, retrieve 0
size=3, default=None, assign: 2=5, retrieve 1
size=4, default=0, assign: 0=3, 2=7, retrieve 2
size=1, assign: 0=9, retrieve 0
size=5, default=-1, assign: 1=0, 4=8, retrieve 3
size=3, default='a', assign: 0='b', 2='c', retrieve 1
size=2, default=0.0, assign: 1=1.5, retrieve 0
size=4, default=None, assign: 3=10, retrieve 3
size=3, default=0, assign: 0=1, 1=2, 2=3, retrieve 1
size=5, default='', assign: 2='x', 4='y', retrieve 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays as Fixed-Size Data Containers
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections with predefined capacity
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by numeric indices starting from 0. The intuition: think of it as a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,..., each holding one item. Key properties: 1) Fixed size: capacity determined at creation, cannot change dynamically. 2) Random access: any element can be accessed directly via index in O(1) time. 3) Homogeneous: typically stores same type of elements. Basic operations: create array of size n, set value at index i, get value at index i. Example: array of size 3 can hold 3 items at indices 0,1,2. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n). Connection to L4: This is the prerequisite knowledge for array operations. Common mistakes: confusing size with index (size=3 means indices 0-2), thinking arrays grow automatically. Real code pattern: In Python, list can simulate array: arr = [None] * 3 creates array of size 3 with None values.

examples.input: Create array of size 3 to store integers, put 10 at index 0, 20 at index 1, 30 at index 2
examples.trace:
1. Create array size 3: starts as [_,_,_] (unknown values)
2. Put 10 at index 0: position 0 becomes 10 → [10,_,_]
3. Put 20 at index 1: position 1 becomes 20 → [10,20,_]
4. Put 30 at index 2: position 2 becomes 30 → [10,20,30]
Indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30

examples.input: Array size 4, default value 0, change index 2 to 5, read index 0 and 2
examples.trace:
1. Create array size 4 with default 0: [0,0,0,0]
2. Change index 2 to 5: set position 2 to 5 → [0,0,5,0]
3. Read index 0: value at position 0 is 0
4. Read index 2: value at position 2 is 5

testCases.input: size=2, put: index0=1, index1=2, read index0
size=3, default=0, put: index1=5, read index2
size=1, put: index0=9, read index0
size=4, default='a', put: index3='z', read index3
size=3, put: index0=10, index2=30, read index1
size=5, default=0.0, put: index4=3.14, read index4
size=2, default=None, put: index1=7, read index0
size=4, put: index0=1, index1=2, index2=3, index3=4, read index2
size=3, default='', put: index2='end', read index2
size=1, default=100, put: index0=50, read index0
